title: Application of the Image-Well Method for Transient Borehole Thermal Energy Storage Systems with Complex Boundaries

research.question: Can an image-well analytical model predict transient borehole thermal energy storage temperatures inside irregular insulated boundaries with useful accuracy and lower computational cost?
research.system: Clusters of borehole heat exchangers inside an irregular two-dimensional insulated BTES boundary, represented by infinite line-source temperature kernels and boundary image nodes.
research.inputData: BHE coordinates and heat rates, ground conductivity and diffusivity, borehole thermal resistance, boundary geometry, heating-cooling schedules, finite-element verification cases, and Drake Landing operating conditions.
research.method: Superpose weighted line-source kernels at BHE and boundary nodes, collocate the no-flux boundary, solve the resulting linear system, apply temporal superposition, and assess parameter sensitivity with the Morris method.
research.benchmark: Four BHE arrangements are compared with finite-element simulations; the reported root-mean-square temperature difference is 0.012 degrees C and computation is reduced by 67 percent.
research.uncertainty: Morris elementary effects rank global parameter influence, but the model assumes infinitely long BHEs and omits seasonal surface, finite-bottom, unsaturated-flow, and buoyancy effects.
research.conclusion: The image-well method reproduces the finite-element temperature field efficiently, supports irregular insulated boundaries and seasonal cycling, and quantifies how boundary representation changes BTES energy estimates.

narrative.throughline: Turn an irregular insulated wall into a ring of thermal image wells whose weights cancel boundary-normal heat flux while preserving the evolving BHE temperature field.
narrative.audienceStartingPoint: An infinite line-source solution gives a fast temperature response for a borehole in an unbounded medium.
narrative.stakes: Real storage fields have irregular insulation boundaries, while repeated meshed simulations can be costly during design and uncertainty analysis.
narrative.resolution: Boundary collocation retains the analytical kernel, enforces the irregular wall through solved image weights, and recovers a continuous transient temperature field.

background.premise: A BTES field functions as a thermal battery that stores surplus heat in boreholes for later recovery.
background.visibleEvidence: A borehole cluster warms during charging and cools during extraction while a seasonal energy meter reverses direction.
background.whyNow: Storage can buffer the intermittency of renewable heat supplies.
background.boundary: The paper analyzes subsurface sensible-heat storage around BHEs rather than electrical battery behavior.
background.sourceExcerpt: These systems use the earth as a thermal battery, storing heat in boreholes during surplus periods and retrieving it when needed
background.motionPurpose: Establish cyclic heat injection and recovery as the original physical object.
background.stepDetail: Pulse heat outward during charging and reverse the pulses during recovery.
background.transitionBridge: The useful kernel is fast, but its default outer geometry is too simple.

background.premise: Standard analytical BTES solutions gain efficiency by imposing simplified boundaries that may not match installed systems.
background.visibleEvidence: A circular or infinite analytical domain fails to align with an irregular insulated perimeter.
background.whyNow: Boundary mismatch changes heat retention and predicted operating temperature.
background.boundary: The limitation concerns the boundary conditions of common analytical solutions, not analytical modeling in general.
background.sourceExcerpt: the applications of analytical solutions can be limited as they invoke simplified boundary conditions that may not reflect typical setups
background.visualObject: Irregular wall over an unbounded kernel field
background.motionPurpose: Expose normal heat-flux arrows crossing a boundary that should insulate.
background.stepDetail: Overlay the real polygonal boundary on an unbounded radial temperature field.
background.transitionBridge: The solution kernel is retained while the boundary is reconstructed with images.

methods.input: A homogeneous conductive medium with a line heat source
methods.output: Transient radial temperature response for one BHE
methods.validity: The adopted kernel assumes an infinitely long line source and the thermal conditions stated in Equations 2-4.
methods.locator: PDF page 4, Section 2.1 Infinite line source solution, Equations 2-4
methods.sourceExcerpt: To limit confounding factors in the model, the infinite line source solution is adopted and subjected to the following conditions
methods.motionPurpose: Grow a continuous thermal halo from one vertical BHE.
methods.stepDetail: Reveal concentric temperature contours around a single line source.
methods.transitionBridge: The dimensional kernel is compressed into reusable scaled coordinates.

methods.output: Dimensionless temperature, Fourier number, radius, and resistance
methods.validity: The scaling uses the paper's heat rate, conductivity, borehole radius, diffusivity, and thermal resistance definitions.
methods.sourceExcerpt: To simplify the model and solution expressions, Equation (7) is made nondimensional. The dimensionless parameters and variables are
methods.motionPurpose: Rescale radius, time, and temperature into one portable kernel.
methods.stepDetail: Replace dimensional axis labels with theta, Fo, delta, and zeta.
methods.transitionBridge: Copies of this kernel can now represent real and image wells.

methods.input: Dimensionless kernels centered on BHE and boundary nodes
methods.validity: Linearity permits superposition under the conductive model and fixed kernel assumptions.
methods.locator: PDF page 5, Section 2.2 Developing a BTES model using the image-well method, Equation 9
methods.sourceExcerpt: the temperature distribution can be expressed as the linear combination of Equation (8) with different coefficients of heat exchange magnitude
methods.visualObject: Real BHE kernels plus boundary-node kernels
methods.motionPurpose: Merge many halos into one composite temperature surface.
methods.stepDetail: Place a kernel at each BHE and image node with an adjustable weight.
methods.transitionBridge: The adjustable boundary kernels need conditions that determine their weights.

methods.output: Linear equations enforcing zero normal heat flux
methods.validity: Boundary accuracy depends on the spatial discretization and placement of boundary nodes.
methods.sourceExcerpt: The discretization of the condition shown in Equation (12) in space is required. The equivalent analytical approach is
methods.motionPurpose: Convert each no-flux boundary marker into one matrix row.
methods.stepDetail: Sample the perimeter and stack normal derivatives of every image kernel at those nodes.
methods.transitionBridge: The completed matrix fixes the unknown boundary weights.

methods.input: Boundary collocation matrix A and BHE forcing vector B
methods.validity: The linear system must be sufficiently resolved and numerically solvable for the chosen node geometry.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Finally, the weights can be solved using linear algebra: (15) Aw = B
methods.motionPurpose: Lock each image halo to its boundary-canceling magnitude.
methods.stepDetail: Propagate the matrix solution back to the perimeter nodes as signed weights.
methods.transitionBridge: The weighted expansion can now be evaluated everywhere inside the store.

methods.input: BHE sources and solved image-well weights
methods.validity: The field applies within the conductive, homogeneous, infinite-BHE model domain.
methods.sourceExcerpt: Subsequently, the spatiotemporal temperature distribution in the BTES region can be evaluated. The procedure to calculate the spatiotemporal temperature distribution
methods.motionPurpose: Fill the insulated polygon with a continuously evolving thermal field.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate the weighted kernel sum on a dense interior grid.
methods.transitionBridge: The continuous field is tested against a meshed numerical reference.

methods.input: Image-well and finite-element temperature distributions for four BHE arrangements
methods.output: Temperature-difference maps against the finite-element benchmark
methods.validity: Agreement is demonstrated for the reported verification scenarios and parameter settings.
methods.locator: PDF page 8, Section 2.3 Model verification, comparison paragraph
methods.sourceExcerpt: temperature distribution generated from Equtaion (9) are compared with results from a standard numerical finite element model
methods.motionPurpose: Reveal residual temperature only where the two fields differ.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate both temperature fields on the same domain; subtract them; display the temperature-difference map.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified pair supplies the matched computational-time comparison in M08.

methods.input: Image-well and finite-element computational times for the reported verification simulations
methods.output: A reported 67 percent reduction for the image-well method
methods.validity: The comparison is the wall-clock result reported for the paper's simulation setup, not a general complexity bound.
methods.locator: PDF page 12, Section 3.1 Assessment of the image-well method for BTES systems
methods.sourceExcerpt: the image‐well method reduced the computational time by 67% compared to the finite element model.
methods.visualObject: Matched finite-element and image-well runtime bars
methods.motionPurpose: Give the reported runtime reduction a dedicated comparison step.
methods.stepDetail: Align the two reported simulation runtimes and mark the 67 percent difference.
methods.transitionBridge: The verified efficient kernel enters Morris sampling in M09.

methods.input: Feasible ranges for BTES model parameters
methods.output: Sampled values for each Morris input factor
methods.validity: Results reflect the feasible ranges and sampling design adopted in the global sensitivity analysis.
methods.locator: PDF page 9, Section 2.4 Global sensitivity analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: requires three steps for each parameter: (1) sampling each parameter
methods.motionPurpose: Select baseline points across each feasible parameter range.
methods.stepDetail: Select one value for every input parameter within its feasible range; retain the sampled factor vector.
methods.transitionBridge: The sampled factors define the perturbation matrix in M10.

methods.input: Sampled parameter points and Morris step size
methods.validity: Each trajectory changes one parameter per step according to the reported binary perturbation design.
methods.locator: PDF pages 9-10, Section 2.4 Global sensitivity analysis
methods.sourceExcerpt: within its feasible range, (2) constructing a perturbation matrix with columns and
methods.motionPurpose: Turn sampled factors into ordered single-axis changes.
methods.stepDetail: Illuminate one changing matrix column in each successive row.
methods.transitionBridge: Every controlled change yields one temperature effect.

methods.output: Temperature predictions for each sampled parameter combination
methods.validity: Equation 9 is evaluated under the paper's 10 x 10 BHE arrangement, selected parameter ranges, 90-day season, and central observation point.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Section 2.4 Morris analysis application, Equation 9
methods.sourceExcerpt: the temperature predictions (output) are computed using Equation (9).
methods.motionPurpose: Run each Morris parameter combination through the verified temperature kernel.
methods.stepDetail: Evaluate Equation 9 once for every row of the Morris design.
methods.transitionBridge: Paired sampled temperatures provide the differences used by Equation 20.

methods.input: Temperature predictions for each sampled parameter combination
methods.output: Elementary effects for each parameter across Morris samples
methods.validity: Equation 20 defines each elementary effect as the temperature-output change divided by the Morris step size.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Section 2.4 Global sensitivity analysis, Equation 20
methods.sourceExcerpt: rows containing 1 and 0, and (3) evaluating the elementary effect on temperature
methods.motionPurpose: Convert each paired temperature change into one elementary-effect value.
methods.stepDetail: Form one Equation 20 difference quotient for every Morris perturbation.
methods.transitionBridge: The collection of effects for each parameter becomes the input to the absolute-mean calculation in M13.

methods.input: Elementary effects for each parameter across Morris samples
methods.output: Mu-star overall-effect coordinate for each parameter
methods.validity: Equation 21 defines mu-star as the absolute mean of the sampled elementary effects.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Section 2.4 Global sensitivity analysis, Equation 21
methods.sourceExcerpt: μ* signifies the overall effect of input parameters on the system
methods.visualObject: Elementary-effect magnitudes condensing onto the Morris horizontal axis
methods.motionPurpose: Assign each parameter its overall-effect coordinate without mixing in variability.
methods.stepDetail: Take the absolute mean of each parameter's elementary effects and place the result on the mu-star axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The same elementary effects supply the standard-deviation coordinate in M14.

methods.input: Elementary effects and their parameter-specific absolute means
methods.output: Sigma interaction coordinate for each parameter
methods.validity: Equation 22 defines sigma from elementary-effect variability and the paper interprets it as nonlinear interaction with other parameters.
methods.locator: PDF page 10, Section 2.4 Global sensitivity analysis, Equation 22
methods.sourceExcerpt: σ represents the nonlinear interaction between the input parameter and others.
methods.visualObject: Elementary-effect spread rising onto the Morris vertical axis
methods.motionPurpose: Assign each parameter its variability coordinate separately from overall effect.
methods.stepDetail: Calculate each parameter's elementary-effect standard deviation and place the result on the sigma axis.
methods.transitionBridge: The completed Morris coordinates give way to seasonal cycling in M15.

methods.input: Heating and cooling start times with fixed BHE heat rates
methods.output: Seasonal charging and discharging temperature history
methods.validity: The superposition assumes the linear conductive kernel and piecewise heat-rate transitions used by the paper.
methods.sourceExcerpt: phase transitions in BHEs are managed using temporal superposition to Equation (9). For example, T (q, t) indicates the heating phase
methods.motionPurpose: Build cooling and reheating from shifted copies of the kernel response.
methods.stepDetail: Add signed response curves at each operating-phase transition.
methods.transitionBridge: Seasonal operation can now be tested inside alternative perimeter shapes in M16.

methods.input: Quadrilateral, octagonal, dodecagonal, and hexadecagonal insulated boundaries
methods.output: Shape-dependent charging and discharging temperature histories
methods.validity: The comparison is limited to the four boundary geometries and parameter scenarios evaluated in the paper.
methods.locator: PDF pages 10-11, Section 2.5 Model application; Figure 5B
methods.sourceExcerpt: four different boundary shapes are evaluated
methods.visualObject: Four boundary outlines beside their charging and discharging temperature traces
methods.motionPurpose: Compare the reported thermal response of the four evaluated geometries.
methods.stepDetail: Hold operating conditions fixed; evaluate each boundary shape; overlay the four temperature histories.
methods.transitionBridge: The boundary-driven temperature difference becomes an energy quantity in M17.

methods.input: Average bounded-versus-unbounded temperature difference with volumetric heat capacity, storage area, and BHE length
methods.output: An additional 3.24 to 16.2 MWh per year for the reported BHE lengths
methods.validity: The calculation applies the paper's octagonal boundary area and the two stated BHE lengths to the mean temperature difference.
methods.locator: PDF page 14, Section 3.4 Case study with heating and cooling cycles
methods.sourceExcerpt: By multiplying the average temperature difference by the volumetric capacity, the area of the
methods.visualObject: Average temperature difference converting into two annual energy bars
methods.motionPurpose: Own the conversion from the modeled temperature difference to the reported storage increment.
methods.stepDetail: Multiply the mean temperature difference by volumetric heat capacity, storage area, and each reported BHE length.
methods.transitionBridge: The owned runtime and energy results return to the insulated Drake Landing field.

return.visualObject: Drake Landing BHE field inside its insulated boundary with seasonal temperature contours and a finite-element residual inset
return.claim: For the reported conductive BTES cases, image-well boundary collocation delivers finite-element-comparable temperatures with substantially lower computational cost and exposes boundary-related storage differences.
return.claimBoundary: The result does not include finite BHE bottoms, seasonal surface forcing, unsaturated thermal coupling, or buoyancy-driven flow, so those settings require a more complete model.
return.locator: PDF page 16, Section 4 Conclusions and Section 3.5 Advantages and limitations of Image-Well method
return.sourceExcerpt: this solution demonstrates comparable accuracy while significantly reducing computational cost by 67%. Additionally, compared to the existing infinite line source solution

visual.primaryObject: BHE cluster inside an irregular insulated boundary
visual.accentObject: Weighted image wells on boundary collocation nodes
visual.geometryNotes: Render BHEs as vertical line sources in plan view, discretize the polygonal insulation perimeter with image nodes, morph temperature halos into a weighted continuous field, and use a timeline for seasonal superposition.
